Kui Buri National Park · Prachuap Khiri Khan, Thailand

Living at the Edge

Elephants move from the forest into these farms most nights. Two records track the conflict: a ranger's logbook and a household survey. This lesson works through both, asking what the data show about how the conflict is changing and who carries its cost.

0Area of the park
230–320Elephants in the forest
0Ranger encounters, 2020–2023
0Households surveyed, 2024
scroll to begin↓
Background

The study site and its two data sources

Ruam Thai village sits on the eastern edge of the park, where smallholdings of pineapple, rubber, mango, and banana meet the forest. Elephants cross into cultivation at night to feed, and rangers drive them back: in this archive, rangers turned elephants back toward the forest at 96% of encounters.

That nightly exchange produced two different records, and this lesson works through both.

The ranger logbook

The Department of National Parks recorded every encounter from 2020 to 2023: how many elephants, what time, where (to a GPS point), and whether crops were damaged. 11,608 rows with usable coordinates.

The household survey

In 2024 the project surveyed 249 households (152 of them farming). It asked who is exposed to raids, who can absorb the loss, who benefits from elephant tourism, and what people think of conservation.

A question to keep in mind throughout: when a recorded number falls, does that always mean the underlying problem got smaller? We return to it at several points.
Part 1 · Spatial and temporal patterns

Where and when conflict occurs

Each dot is one elephant encounter, dropped at its GPS coordinate. Bigger dot, bigger group. Warm dots happened after dark; cool dots in daylight. Red flares are encounters where crops were damaged. The outlines are the five sectors rangers use to organize the area. The frame holds the roughly 11,160 encounters around the village; a few hundred scattered records sit off-screen.

2020
Year
0
Encounters
0
Mean group
0%
Lone elephants

Just over half of all encounters (6,125 of 11,608, 53%) fall inside the five sectors, and they are unevenly distributed: sector 2 around the main village has the highest intensity at 2.3 encounters per hectare, while sector 3 to the south has 0.7. Encounters concentrate in a narrow band along the forest edge.

The 2020 records are incomplete. 2020 was the program's start-up year; first-quarter counts are about half those of later years because monitoring was still being established. The rise from 2020 to 2021 mostly reflects improved recording rather than more elephants, so the trend is read from 2021 onward.

When do most raids occur?

After dark.

Of the 9,717 encounters with a recorded time, 96% occurred at night, concentrated between 18:00 and 02:00, in every year. Movement concentrates when human activity at the fields is lowest.

When raids happen, by hour

Time-stamped encounters by hour of day (n = 9,717). Shaded band marks 18:00–06:00. Source: DNP encounter archive, 2020–2023.
Part 2 · Group size over time

A shift toward solitary raiding

illustrative image

Encounters stayed in the thousands every year, so the total count alone suggests little change. The composition of those encounters tells a different story.

Across 2020 to 2023, the raiding groups got…

Smaller. Raiding shifted from groups toward single elephants.

Mean raiding-group size fell from 3.2 to 2.2 elephants, and the share of encounters involving a single elephant rose from 37% to 60%. By 2023, a majority of recorded encounters involved one elephant.

Mean group size and solo share by year

Left: mean group size by year. Right: share of encounters that were a single elephant, for all encounters and for foot-patrol encounters only. n = 11,072 encounters with a recorded count. Source: DNP archive.

Asian elephant crop-raiding is disproportionately the work of adult males, which range farther from the family group and take greater risks at cultivated land (Sukumar & Gadgil, 1988). The log records no sex, so this is an inference rather than a measurement, but the solitary, calf-free encounters that now dominate the record are most consistent with adult males.

A detection check. Could the trend reflect who recorded the encounters rather than the elephants themselves? Watch points record smaller groups than foot patrols, and the mix of the two channels shifted over the period. Restricting to foot-patrol encounters, the solo share still rises from 29% to 53%, so the shift is not an artifact of detection.
Part 3 · Reading the damage record

Interpreting the decline in recorded damage

The share of encounters with recorded crop damage went 11%, 14%, 12%, then 5%. The 2023 value is the lowest in the record, and it is tempting to read it as the conflict easing.

Recorded damage dropped sharply. Does that mean the conflict eased?

Not necessarily. Two factors lower the recorded rate independent of any change in elephant behaviour.

Crop-damage rate by year

Share of encounters with recorded crop damage, by year. The 2022→2023 drop overlaps a change in the recording form (see below). Source: DNP archive (n = 11,608).

1. The composition of raiders changed

Solitary encounters carry crop damage less often (4.2%) than small groups (about 10%). As single elephants came to dominate, the average damage rate fell for that reason alone, independent of any behavioural change. The effect is real but small: adjusting for it moves the per-year odds ratio only from 0.74 to 0.77.

Crop-damage rate by group size

Probability an encounter involved crop damage, by group size, with 95% intervals and sample sizes. Source: DNP archive.

2. The recording form changed

The steepest part of the decline falls between 2022 and 2023, the same year the inspection form changed: fields for accompanying gaur and for calves are blank through 2022 and begin to carry entries in 2023. When the recording protocol changes in the year the number breaks, part of the decline may reflect how damage was recorded rather than how much occurred.

Key point

A falling count is not self-interpreting. Before reading a decline as success, ask two questions: has the composition of what is counted changed, and has the recording method changed?

Where and when damage occurs

Damage is not concentrated at the forest edge. Most elephants are intercepted there; the encounters that cause damage are the minority that penetrate into cultivation. The crop-damage rate rises from 6% within 100 m of the boundary to 39% beyond one kilometre.

Crop-damage rate by distance into cultivation

Crop-damage rate by distance from the sector boundary into cultivation. Source: DNP archive, Section 4.6.

The busiest months are not the costliest. Encounters peak from October to December, but the damage rate peaks in February and May, when fewer elephants arrive but more mature crops are in the ground. Damage tracks what is planted, not how often elephants appear.

Encounters and damage rate by month

Bars: encounters per month (all years pooled). Line: crop-damage rate per month. Source: DNP archive.
Part 4 · Sector-level variation

Variation across sectors

Four of the five sectors moved toward smaller groups and less damage. Sector 3 did not: it held a mean group size of 3.6 and a damage rate near 19% in both 2021 and 2023, retaining the herd-scale conflict the others lost.

Group size and damage rate by sector, 2021 → 2023

Each line connects a sector's 2021 value to its 2023 value. Sector 3 (highlighted) barely moves on either measure. Source: DNP archive, Table 3.
Why the average matters. Planning from the frontier-wide trend would suggest the herd problem is receding and herd-scale measures can be scaled back. Sector 3 is the exception that decision would miss. Averages summarize; they also conceal local variation.
Part 5 · Household vulnerability

Measuring household vulnerability

illustrative image

The ranger log records elephants, not which households can absorb a lost harvest. The 2024 survey of 152 farming households addresses that, scoring each household on three dimensions.

Exposure

How much conflict reaches the farm: proximity to the forest edge, raid frequency, and whether raiding is increasing.

Sensitivity

How much a raid hurts: dependence on farm income, whether elephant-attractive crops are grown, income instability, and household composition.

Adaptive capacity

Buffers against loss: total income, income diversification, deterrents, contacts with officials, and group membership.

One might expect households to fall into discrete types, such as a high-risk type and a secure type. They do not. A latent class analysis returns a single class, and the three dimensions are nearly independent: a household's position on one is a poor predictor of its position on the others. Vulnerability here is a continuous gradient rather than a set of types.

152 households along the exposure gradient

Each dot is one farming household, placed by its exposure score. The distribution is continuous, with no separation into clusters. Source: 2024 household survey.
Part 6 · Distribution of benefits

Who receives the tourism benefit

illustrative image

Kui Buri runs elephant tourism on the same population that raids the farms, and tourism income is intended to offset the cost of living alongside elephants. 28% of farming households received a tourism benefit. The question is which households.

Who do you think received the tourism benefit?

The benefit tracked household resources, not exposure to conflict.

Grouped into thirds by adaptive capacity, the share receiving a benefit rises from 8% to 45%. Grouped by exposure, it does not increase (18%, 37%, 27%). The benefit follows social and economic position rather than need.

Tourism benefit received, by household third

Share receiving a tourism benefit across thirds of adaptive capacity (green) and exposure (amber). n = 152 farming households. Source: 2024 survey.

In a regression, each one-standard-deviation step up in capacity roughly doubles the odds of receiving a benefit (odds ratio 2.01, 95% CI 1.31–3.08), and it survives correction for testing many outcomes. Exposure and sensitivity predict nothing. The effect runs through group membership and having more than one income source, not raw income.

Predictors of receiving the tourism benefit

Odds ratios per +1 SD (dot) with 95% intervals (line). Right of 1 = more likely to receive the benefit. Only capacity clears the line. Source: 2024 survey, audited estimates.

This is what environmental-justice scholars term a distributive problem (Schlosberg, 2007): a benefit intended to offset harm flows to the households best positioned to claim it rather than to those bearing the most damage. The households at the forest edge are also the least likely to view conservation as beneficial.

Conservation viewed as beneficial, by exposure third

Share viewing conservation as beneficial, by exposure third. Falls from 78% to 57% as exposure rises (OR 0.52 per +1 SD, p = 0.002). Exploratory: exposure and attitude come from one self-report instrument. Source: 2024 survey.
Part 7 · Comparing the two records

Survey reports and the ranger record

illustrative image

68% of households said conflict got worse over five years. The ranger log does not tell the same story: in-sector encounters ran 1,613 → 2,077 → 1,692 across 2021 to 2023, and recorded damage fell in 2023. The months people name as worst do not line up with the log's busy months either.

Perceived change vs recorded encounters

Bars: in-sector encounters per year (the record). Marker: 68% of households perceived an increase (lived experience). The two traces pull apart. Source: DNP archive + 2024 survey.

The two are not measuring the same thing, and neither is simply wrong. A household experiences the single raid that destroys a season's crop; the log counts every encounter, most of which end without damage. The two agree on crops: pineapple is about 36% of both cultivation and recorded damage, rubber appears in damage more than its share of planting (35% of damage against 23% of cultivation), and mango is widely grown but rarely damaged.

Crops grown vs crops damaged

Share of cultivation (survey) vs share of recorded damage (log) for each crop, in-sector. Rubber is over-represented in damage; mango under-represented. Source: 2024 survey + DNP archive.
The disagreement is itself informative. When two records diverge, the question is not which is correct but what each one measures. Self-reported experience and recorded incidents capture different aspects of the same conflict, and both are needed.
Summary

Summary and key points

This lesson worked through two real datasets on one village. Five points generalize beyond this case.

01

A stable total can hide structural change

The number of encounters stayed in the thousands; what changed was the composition, from herds to single elephants.

02

A declining number is not automatically progress

A change in what is counted, or in how it is recorded, can move a metric without an underlying change. The 2023 decline reflects both.

03

Averages can conceal local variation

The frontier-wide trend suggested herds were declining; sector 3 retained the herd-scale problem throughout.

04

Benefits may not reach those bearing the cost

Tourism income tracked household resources, not exposure. Who a program reaches should be measured, not assumed.

05

Disagreement between datasets is information

The logbook and the survey measure different aspects of the conflict. Use both rather than treating either as ground truth.

Discussion. With a fixed budget for the coming year, would you prioritize reducing damage, directing the tourism benefit toward the most exposed households, or improving monitoring so future figures can be trusted? Support your answer with the data above.
Methods and limitations

What the analysis can and cannot show

This is one village and one cultivation frontier: a detailed case, not a law of nature. The four-year trend rests on 2021–2023, because 2020 was a recording ramp-up. The lone raiders are inferred to be bulls from elephant behaviour and the near-absence of calves; the log records no sex, so a few could be young or displaced animals. The 2023 protocol change limits the damage trend directly, which is why the 2023 figure is treated as uncertain rather than as a clean estimate.

The survey is a single 2024 snapshot, so these are associations, not proof of cause. The exposure-to-attitude link shares one self-report instrument with its outcome, so it is reported as exploratory; the coexistence version is weaker still and is suggestive only. Sector boundaries were digitized from a georeferenced field map and are accurate to tens of metres. An attempt to validate self-reported exposure against the incident record failed, because 104 of 147 mapped farmers sit in a single sector and the log is weighted toward where rangers patrol; the household-level signal cannot be rebuilt from sector averages.

Data: Department of National Parks elephant-encounter archive, Kui Buri, 2020–2023 (11,608 georeferenced records); household survey, Ruam Thai, 2024 (249 respondents, Qualtrics). Every map and chart on this page was recomputed from the source data and reconciled against the project's audited reference.

Sources cited: Schlosberg, D. (2007). Defining Environmental Justice: Theories, Movements, and Nature. Oxford University Press.  Sukumar, R. & Gadgil, M. (1988). Male-female differences in foraging on crops by Asian elephants. Animal Behaviour, 36(4), 1233–1235.

A note on the images: the full-screen banner images that open several sections are illustrative (AI-generated) and set mood only; they are not photographs of Ruam Thai or its residents. The maps, charts, and every number come from the project data.

Living at the Edge · A scrollytelling lesson on human-elephant conflict, data literacy, and environmental justice at Kui Buri National Park, Thailand.

Built for teaching at the School for Field Studies. Chapter 3 fieldwork, Tyler Nuckols. All visuals run on the real project data, embedded directly in this file: no internet connection required.
